comments:
Summary:
The paper proposes to learn type-II (output-space) opposites for opposition-based learning via artificial neural networks. The method consists of two stages: (1) "opposition mining" that constructs (quasi-)opposite input examples by mapping each observed output y to an opposite output oppY according to one of several simple schemes (ymax+ymin-y, mean-based, etc.) and selecting the input whose observed output is closest to oppY; (2) training an ANN to predict the mined type-II opposite x̆II as a function of x. Experiments on several simple benchmark functions and three standard optimization problems compare the ANN approach against an evolving fuzzy inference systems (FIS) baseline; results are reported to favor ANNs in most cases. The authors discuss limitations in the presence of highly non-monotonic mappings and suggest piecewise decomposition as future work.

Strengths:
- The paper addresses a concrete, well-defined problem in opposition-based learning (approximating type-II opposites) and presents a straightforward, implementable approach.
- The opposition-mining + regression idea is intuitive and the provided Algorithm 1 clarifies the pipeline.
- The authors acknowledge important limitations (surjectivity and non-monotonicity) and test on both function-approximation and optimization tasks.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- Experimental detail is insufficient for reproducibility. Key details missing include ANN architecture(s), training procedure (optimizer, learning rate, epochs, regularization), dataset sizes beyond a few mentions, how hyperparameters were selected, and exact statistical test results. Reported claims of statistical significance are not supported by presented numbers or confidence intervals in the provided text.
- Baselines are limited. Comparison is only to an evolving FIS method from prior work. It would strengthen the paper to include simpler and stronger baselines: e.g., direct regression to invert y→x (where possible), kernel regression, random-forest regressors, or even more modern regressors; also performance relative to type-I opposites is mentioned but not rigorously analyzed as an ablation.
- The opposition schemes (T1/T2/T3) are ad-hoc and not well justified. No comparative analysis explains why one scheme works better for which classes of functions, beyond superficial reporting that T1 often performs best.
- The opposition-mining step can fail or produce ambiguous mappings when f is non-monotonic or when the dataset does not contain close samples for oppY; while the issue is noted, the remedy (piecewise monotonic decomposition) is only mentioned as future work. There is no detailed analysis of how frequently mining produces poor or noisy labels and how that affects ANN training.
- The benchmarks used are described as intentionally simple and mostly monotonic; hence the experimental regime avoids the toughest cases and limits claims about general utility in realistic, complex problems.
- Presentation suffers from repetitions and typographical issues in the provided text sample; figures/tables referenced (e.g., Table 1 and 2, Fig. 1) are not shown in-line so it is hard to verify reported results.

Recommendations for Improvement:
1. Add full experimental details: network architectures, hyperparameter settings, training/validation splits, random seeds, and complete numerical results (means, standard deviations, p-values) for all comparisons. Include the referenced tables and figures in the paper body.
2. Broaden baselines: include standard regression methods and simple inversion schemes to show the specific advantage (if any) of ANNs over generic regressors for this task. Provide ablations comparing training on mined labels vs. synthetic exact opposites when available.
3. Analyze robustness of the opposition-mining step: quantify how often oppY has no close match and how the choice of "closest y" affects label noise. Explore interpolation strategies (e.g., interpolate inputs for exact oppY) rather than snapping to the closest sample.
4. Investigate more challenging, non-monotonic functions (or present the piecewise decomposition method and results) to show how the approach scales to realistic problems; report runtime/complexity.
5. Clarify and motivate the opposition schemes and, if possible, propose a principled way to choose the scheme depending on data characteristics.
6. Clean up presentation, remove repeated paragraphs and ensure all referenced materials (tables/figures) are present and readable.

Overall assessment:
The paper presents an incremental but sensible application of ANNs to learn approximate type-II opposites, and it may be of interest to researchers working on opposition-based learning heuristics. However, at present the manuscript lacks experimental rigor and sufficient methodological detail to fully support its claims. With more complete experiments, clearer presentation, and broader baselines, the work would be substantially strengthened.
